sentences.text: Yeah, escaped.
And mostly Jew.
In Kowel, when I'm in Kowel, a lot of people, Jewish people, [INAUDIBLE], escaped from Poland from the Germans.
And I'm there maybe [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] six weeks.
And I had registered to go [INAUDIBLE] to work because in this town people died, a lot of people.
No where to live, not a house.
There's nothing, you know.
And one day I went [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to the train, a Pullman train, not a passenger train.
It take maybe 5,000 people, the train.
They take to Russia, and they get you nothing to eat on the road, and nowhere to sit.
You sit on the floor.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I come south.
The train stopped in Berdichev.
This is a town in Russian, in Russian Ukraine, Berdichev.
There, the train got staying for hours.
A lot of people went out to the station, train station.
And one Russian man comes over to me said, you want to eat something?
I said, yes.
They get me a soup.
What they have soup there from sour pickles.
In my life, I don't eat this.
I am hungry, but I can't eat it.
The other man comes over, said, come, I give you have something else.
He gave me a little meat.
And later the train went all night till the morning.
In the morning, I come in a town which is [INAUDIBLE].
The name.
It was just a mine for marble.
You know marble?
Mm-hm.
Mm-hm.
So this are stones there.
And they located the young people to physical work.
The older people with the-- with the women with children take the coal horses to farm.
Yeah.
In this mine, I have a very bad time.
Don't get you to eat too much, and you work so hard.
You take that stones, marble, from the mine, and I have with other five boys from Poland-- they take this five kilometers to the other station, to push this.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] There, I stayed in this place maybe [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] three month.
And from this place [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] take me to a town [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] called Melitopol.
So that city, maybe there's 60,000, 70,000 people in the city lived.
This is-- but the [INAUDIBLE] with cream, like here, Florida.
Warm, beautiful there.
And there I start [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to work like a tailor, like a furrier, you know?
My professional-- Had you learned this from your father?
My father, yeah.
And this is a shop in the government-- everything belonged there to the government-- used especially for the city hall.
[SIGHS] And I remember [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't get letters from my parents.
I thought that my parents not even know where I am.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to the post office, and I'm lucky I find there a Polish Russian worker.
She work in the post office.
She tell me, you know what?
In two months, maybe six weeks, you can send letters to Poland.
Well, the Russians with Germany maked a pact, you know?
You know what means?
An arrangement.
And I sent letters to my parents and I get back.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I sent them-- my mother and my father asked me for satin and for a little [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] flour to make breads or something, you know?
I sent them-- somebody try and send it to me-- you know, I sent them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this go on till 1941.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I'm satisfied there.
I work and this is very warm there.
I can get fluids, you know.
For, like, [INAUDIBLE].
But the [INAUDIBLE] them with cream.
Cream that's like Florida.
[SIGHS] One day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a telephone to the shop, Michael Steinstock has to come over to the city hall, room eight.
I don't know what was going on.
Anything can happen.
I don't know what.
I'm not afraid, because I not do nothing, you know?
I went there, I knocked the door, somebody met me, a man, a heavy man.
You're Michael?
Yeah.
Sit down.
Now, he asked me, are you from Poland?
Yeah.
From Lublin?
Yes.
You have a father, a mother there?
Sisters?
Uncles?
A brother?
I'm all, yes.
There's a paper, write down how's the name for your grandfather and your grandmother, for my father, my mother, my sisters, my brother was.
Everything else.
Where you have the letters what you get from Poland?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I told him I lived with somebody in a room and I can make [INAUDIBLE] the room.
When I read the-- the letters, I throw them out.
I have in the drawer, I have copies of my letters.
You had copies?
Mm-hm.
You didn't tell them that?
No, The, the men have the copies.
Yeah, and this man is from the NKVD.
The [INAUDIBLE].
This is the Russian Gestapo.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What happened was the two brothers [?
Dorfman ?] what I escaped with them, is together with me in the same city.
Because in the beginning I told you--
These two brothers went back to Poland, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] went back to Poland to Germany because the-- the two brothers, they couldn't take this life.
The Russians were very hard to live.
But for his parents, a little richer, he can make, you know, it's a better life--
Here, you have to work.
For me, it is not so hard because I'm from a poor house, poor parents.
And this older brother, [?
Dorfman ?] sent me a letter in German.
[INAUDIBLE] gone to factory, and-- in a metal-- a metal factory, and learn to make legs and go away.
You know what this means?
Escape in Russia and go back to Germany.
The Russian Gestapo is smart.
Oh, he didn't fool them.
He said, your friend is a spy.
Are you a spy?
I told him, how am I a spy?
I'm a young boy, and I'm a Jewish boy.
No, you're a Polish pig.
[LAUGHS]
Sign you're a spy.
I'm not a spy.
I don't know what you talk to me.
Arrested me and put me on a cellar.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] [SOBS]
Every-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] every night, 2 o'clock in the morning, They would-- sign.
[LAUGHS] I don't sign.
I know if I sign, this it-- this it is, go and hang me.
And this man, I remember this man's name.
Shestabitov. ?] Because somebody called-- not of one is [INAUDIBLE], this is prison already.
They're going to take me out, that they have four or five here they've-- they uncovered there from the Gestapo.
And one called, [?
Shestabitov, ?] like this with the finger.
And one time, they put my [INAUDIBLE] to the nose.
[SOBBING]
[SOBBING] Electric, they put electric.
In three days, I lost hair, in three days.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm lucky.
The Germans started the war in 1941, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the Germans go so fast.
They came to the city where I live and you can hear, like, the artillery through the city.
It's three kilometers from that city to Germany.
And the guards from the prison escaped and never barred the prisoners.
Broke out the doors.
They went to the office in the prison-- of course, in Russia everybody ought to-- have the passport, a passport.
You know this.
And there where you [INAUDIBLE] till you were sitting in jail to-- and I have the Article 58 because I am from Poland.
It-- in there, social [INAUDIBLE] element.
This mean I'm a dangerous person.
Yeah, in the passport.
And now, everybody escaped, and I escaped, too.
With the [INAUDIBLE] would go out in the city, a lot of skins to make shoes, and I jump there and the train go [INAUDIBLE] to Russia.
To the train
I saved my life.
And the train comes to a city called Kazan.
Kazan.
K-A-Z-A-N.
And I have-- I met there two boys, three boys from Poland, Jewish boys.
What?
Met them there.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there is not coats.
It's so cold.
Very expensive-- I didn't have a cent in the pocket.
That come to the Russian [INAUDIBLE].
Russian [INAUDIBLE]?
Uzbekistan.
Maybe you've heard of Uzbekistan?
Kazakhstan?
To see-- those belonged to Russia.
You have to go over the-- the desert.
Only sand and then the sky.
You go three-- three nights and three days.
No, no, with the train.
Oh, on the train.
How you can go on-- you don't see nothing.
Sand and the sky.
Nothing.
And I come to Tashkent.
Tashkent was a capital city from Uzbekistan.
It was a Muslim country.
All this belonged to Russia, to the Communists.
And there, people-- there's very warm around here.
People lived in the street and slept in the cities, all Jews who escaped and all Russians come there.
Sleeping in the streets and dying in the streets, you know?
And the people, I'm sorry, smelled [INAUDIBLE] because they-- where to wash?
Were nothing.
You have a sign in the street, everyone go to the farm.
I went and I-- I-- I want go because on the farm I get a little bread.
I can wash myself, you know?
Took three boys, my new friends, which I escaped with them.
And I one day come, it was called in Uzbek [INAUDIBLE] language, [INAUDIBLE].
This is a little-- a little woods, make like this, and a little-- how do call this?
The little-- with the long ears.
Donkey?
An animal?
An animal.
The one-- the little one.
Maybe a donkey.
Maybe a donkey.
I never hard this word till now.
And it comes on its back a Muslim man.
And he taked the four boys, the-- I and--
Three boys more, yeah.
Solomon-- yeah.
And they are in the little wagon.
And and the little-- this was so high.
The tires were so high, like, one floor.
So high.
I walked all the time.
Sand and the sky.
Sand and the sky.
It taked all day to come in the [INAUDIBLE].
This is a-- a-- a farm.
The name is and the Stalin's [INAUDIBLE].
In the meantime--
What, now listen, in the-- when you went there to the farm, you see little houses.
Not built houses, mostly made from [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] straw with something like this.
You don't have a door.
You go in, like, an opening.
No windows, a little something.
And stopped a lot of places, very poor places.
And at one place, we are come in a farm.
I see building there.
Beautiful there.
And a boy, like the Uzbeks-- dark, you know, like the Indian look, comes over and said, you're Jewish?
Yeah.
You speak-- this boy-- one boy speaked Russian, but the Uzbeks from there don't speak Russia.
They speak this language.
Mm-hm.
I said-- here, [INAUDIBLE], this farm is Jewish.
I told to him Hebrew, [SPEAKING HEBREW]?
How's your name?
He tell me right away.
Very nice boy.
He bring something to eat.
Now, later, I moved to this [INAUDIBLE] where I have the--
No.
No, I go farther from there.
Not in the Jewish, no.
I come there, only Uzbek.
See, this is Muslim men.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You want to have where to stay?
Where to sleep?
I get you a place, like a hole you go in.
No floor, and a rock like this.
What we can do?
We stayed there.
The next day, get you something to eat.
You go work in the-- in the field, which make the cigarettes.
You know the-- how you call this?
Tobacco.
I worked by this, very hard.
The people from there, the Uzbeks, have here something.
Who cared for me?
Nobody cared for you.
They get you Every day, get you a little potatoes and that's it.
And they're so hard.
I staying there six months.
In 19-- I don't want to tell what was happened there.
And one day, I and my new friend escaped from there.
When they catch you, you go to Siberia.
Oh, yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I went to the train and I no have a ticket.
A-- I jump in, the four boys, and the train go back to Russian-- to Russian-- to the--
The controller was-- was continuing to look do you have a ticket.
It was a Russia girl.
What's going to happen then?
And the one friend of mine, the name is Carlo, from Warsaw, said, don't worry.
He had a beautiful voice and started to song.
This girl controller, she soon forget, and she didn't ask for the ticket.
I come over the catacomb, the-- the desert, it's 15 below the zero already.
Here, it's so hot.
And the one from the boys-- the name is Solomon-- taked a little-- how call this?
A plate, you know?
I said, you staying in the train, the train staying in the-- in the station 15, 20 minutes, I'll go take us soup for everybody, and go buy us soup.
I went there and [INAUDIBLE] the train left.
Maybe four stations later, the-- the police comes in.
Papers.
I have my passport.
Now, there is not a stamp in the jail before.
You know, my [INAUDIBLE].
Mm-hm.
They taked out the four boys-- no, the three boys.
One was there in-- was went to the restaurant.
The three boys are take to the police, and the manager from the police is-- sit down here.
Here are two policemen.
He called, you come in there.
My one friend go in.
And screamed him, you deserve this.
You could hang.
I come in to him.
I told him.
You didn't hear talk like this.
No.
This man taked the passports from me and from my two friends, and went somewhere, and locked the door.
And the policeman is a very nice man.
He said, you have something to eat?
He eats [INAUDIBLE].
When they come back, you only can eat.
I'll come back in an hour.
He called the policeman.
The policeman said, come on, to everybody.
He taked out, and the policeman, follow me.
The policeman's in front, and I'm with my friends in the back.
The snow's so high.
That's [INAUDIBLE] now it's OK.
Nobody's going to kill you right now.
And taked to the Army office.
There, hundreds of people take [INAUDIBLE].
You wait here.
Now, called me.
I come in.
About 15 officers and a table.
Stalin hanging, you know, this-- with-- Michael, you want go to the Army?
I'm, yes, I want go.
Good, you're a soldat.
You are a soldier, you know?
You tried to tell you no want to go, they cut you off the head.
I went to the Army.
Yeah.
In beginning.
This is in January.
Yeah.
And I take to a place in the woods.
It's over-- under there, special maked, you know?
Because the Germans couldn't see.
You slept in the woods in the dirt makes-- there's not beds.
Nothing.
Woods.
Everybody sleep like this.
Cold The wool is with ice so thick.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I stayed there maybe [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] three month.
Get you every day one little bread, dry bread.
You have to go out.
You take hot water yourself.
This is till 1 o'clock.
You walk every day deep in the woods.
You cut off a tree, you bring over your shoulders back and build another place on the dirt, you know?
It's called [INAUDIBLE].
What?
I said, not an easy life.
It-- so cold, 50 below the zero.
And one day, it's 12 o'clock at night, a sargeant comes in-- there's 200 people in that one place-- Michael-- quiet, everything there go quiet.
This, this, this, this, out.
Take that, a lot of soldiers.
It's 12 o'clock, midnight.
Everybody's stand in line.
Nobody asked what's going to happen.
You know can-- this is the Army.
They are disciplined.
You walk all night.
The officers at the front, and you walk.
In the morning you come in a station, a train station.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When the train station takes you [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] to a place called [INAUDIBLE].
And there, everybody's out.
And this is a village, a big village.
And the officers opened the door for the farmer.
How many people live there?
Two.
And one soldier here.
Here two people.
Here three people.
Nobody can tell [INAUDIBLE].
You sleep on the floor.
You understand?
Very hard life there.
And how long did that continue?
And then from there, stayed, I come to Stalingrad.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I come to Stalingrad with the division which I belong.
I don't want to tell you the time what I stayed in the villages, a lot of villages.
It's-- it's a long-- a long story, know, you know?
No, I'll tell.
Night come, to [?
Kamyshin ?] [?
Kamyshin, ?] this is 35 kilometers.
This was about 20 miles from Stalingrad.
They stopped the Army, the division line and from there, they get you [INAUDIBLE] this is wool, make shoes so thick.
Get you a very thick with cotton inside-- warm-- to go to Stalingrad.
